# Cell-Cell Interactions in Neurodegenerative diseases: Models and Biotherapies (CEA)

![](https://research.pasteur.fr/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/research_pasteur-enanselme-perrier-cea-research-pasteur-enanselme-perrier-cea-photo-id-anselme-2019b1-150x150.jpg)

## [Anselme Perrier (CEA)](https://research.pasteur.fr/en/epartner/anselme-perrier-cea/)

![](https://research.pasteur.fr/wp-content/uploads/2015/09/research.pasteur.fr_logo_cea_2012_pleinetaille-150x150.jpg)

### About

**Research focus and objectives of the team**

Our working hypothesis is that the selective neuronal dysfunctions and death occurring in many neurodegenerative diseases originate not only from neuron-intrinsic impairments but also from the convergence of dysfunctions and damages developed within multiple cell types in specific brain areas. This includes synaptic interactions between the most vulnerable neurons within neuronal networks (e.g. striatal and cortical neurons in Huntington’s disease), as well as functional interactions between these neurons and neighboring glial cells such as astrocytes or microglia. In order to interrogate the respective role of specific cell populations, we are developing innovative in cellulo (rodent primary neural cultures and human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPS)) and in vivo (rodents and non-human primates with Team 6) models of Huntington’s, Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease with viral vectors. In parallel we make use of the biological resources, tools and models created to design, develop and assess new biotherapies including in particular gene and substitutive cell therapy approaches. Overall, we aim at:

**1.Investigate the molecular basis of regional vulnerability in neurodegenerative diseases**

**2. Elucidate the role of neuron-glia interactions during the course of neurodegeneration**

**3.Design and assess innovative biotherapies for neurodegenerative diseases**

[Team website](https://jacob.cea.fr/drf/ifrancoisjacob/english/Pages/Departments/MIRCen/ResearchThemes/cell-interactions-biotherapies-models.aspx)

​Research focus and objectives of the team

Our working hypothesis is that the selective neuronal dysfunctions and death occurring in many neurodegenerative diseases originate not only from neuron-intrinsic impairments but also from the convergence of dysfunctions and damages developed within multiple cell types in specific brain areas. This includes synaptic interactions between the most vulnerable neurons within neuronal networks (e.g. striatal and cortical neurons in Huntington's disease), as well as functional interactions between these neurons and neighboring glial cells such as astrocytes or microglia. In order to interrogate the respective role of specific cell populations, we are developing innovative in cellulo (rodent primary neural cultures and human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPS)) and in vivo (rodents and non-human primates with Team 6) models of Huntington's, Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease with viral vectors. In parallel we make use of the biological resources, tools and models created to design, develop and assess new biotherapies including in particular gene and substitutive cell therapy approaches. Overall, we aim at:

**1.Investigate the molecular basis of regional vulnerability in neurodegenerative diseases**

**2. Elucidate the role of neuron-glia interactions during the course of neurodegeneration**

**3.Design and assess innovative biotherapies for neurodegenerative diseases**

A new research theme has recently been added. It focuses on glial interactions during human brain development.​ You will find below the summary of three research projects that are ongoing in the team.

## ​Glia Interactions during Human Brain Development and Epilepsy

**Principal Investigator : Dr. Giampaolo Milior**

Traditionally, brain function has been attributed primarily to neuronal activity, yet growing evidence underscores the critical role of glial cells as astrocytes and  microglia in synaptic regulation.

The concept of the multipartite synapse extends beyond the traditional tripartite model, recognizing how glia actively modulate synaptic strength, neurotransmission, and circuit integration. Astrocytes regulate synaptic activity through gliotransmitter release, microglia sculpt neural circuits via synaptic pruning and metabolic sensing, influencing synaptic efficacy. The emerging view suggests that neuron-glia interactions are central not only for synaptic communication but also for developmental processes such as brain network maturation. Understanding these complex, reciprocal interactions in a holistic framework is crucial for deciphering how the brain establishes and refines its functional architecture in both physiological and pathological conditions.

However, these complex synaptic mechanisms have been revealed primarily through studies in rodent models, and our understanding of how they operate in the **human brain** remains limited. The unique features of human brain development and the diversity of its cellular interactions call for complementary approaches.

Our research focuses on uncovering **novel neuron-glia interactions** using innovative **3D human brain organotypic****models** derived from human diseased brain tissues. These models offer a unique opportunity to explore how glial cells contribute to synaptic function and network maturation in the human brain, particularly in contexts relevant to **neurodevelopmental disorders and epilepsies**. By bridging the gap between rodent studies and human physiology, this work aims to identify human-specific mechanisms that may underlie pathological processes and open new avenues for therapeutic intervention.​

*(A) Two-photon microscopy image showing live human microglia (green) interacting with neurons (magenta).*

*(B) STED microscopy revealing fine synaptic interactions between microglia and both presynaptic (magenta) and postsynaptic (white) elements (indicated by the arrow).*

*(C) Physiological patch clamp recordings in human brain slice preparations showing neuronal activity and synaptic currents during microglia motilities. ​*

## Role of glial cells in tauopathies

**Tauopathies are characterized by the progressive accumulation of abnormal forms of Tau protein in the brain**. Most often, Tau aggregates are found in neurons, but in some tauopathies, Tau aggregates are also detected in astrocytes. Yet both their origin and the consequences of their accumulation are unknown.

In collaboration with Université de Lille, we developed rodent models of tauopathies overexpressing tau variants with a distinct propensity to aggregate. We showed that astrocytic Tau pathology can be secondary to neuronal pathology. Using new gene transfer-based tools, we also demonstrated a transfer of Tau between neurons and astrocytes. We found severe toxicity of soluble hyperphosphorylated forms of Tau for specific populations of astrocytes. Our data suggest that astrocytes are not mere bystanders of neuronal pathology and that Tau pathology in astrocytes may significantly contribute to clinical symptoms.

![](/drf/ifrancoisjacob/PublishingImages/departements-services/MIRCen/tauopathie.png)
![](file:///C:/Users/rj233114/AppData/Local/Temp/msohtmlclip1/01/clip_image003.gif)

![](file:///C:/Users/rj233114/AppData/Local/Temp/msohtmlclip1/01/clip_image005.jpg)Figure 1 : *Our working hypothesis
is based on the bi-directional exchange of pathological Tau protein between
neurons and astrocytes (left). Confocal image showing the presence of
hyperphosp​horylated Tau (AT100, red) in neurons and in one astrocyte (GFAP
green, white ​​​arrow) in 18-month old Thy-Tau22- mouse (right)*.

![](file:///C:/Users/rj233114/AppData/Local/Temp/msohtmlclip1/01/clip_image005.jpg)Figure 1 : *Our working hypothesis
is based on the bi-directional exchange of pathological Tau protein between
neurons and astrocytes (left). Confocal image showing the presence of
hyperphosp​horylated Tau (AT100, red) in neurons and in one astrocyte (GFAP
green, white ​​​arrow) in 18-month old Thy-Tau22- mouse (right)*.  

Along with astrocytes, microglial cells are involved in the neuroinflammatory response to Tauopathy. The variants of TREM2, a cell surface receptor expressed by microglia, confer a risk of developing AD comparable to the ε4 allele of the APOE gene. This suggests that in addition to responding to the pathological process, these glial cells may actually play a key role in the emergence of the disease. We investigated the impact of TREM2 deficiency on the severity of Tauopathy in the THY-Tau22 transgenic line, a well-characterized model of tauopathy. We found an increase in the severity of tauopathy in TREM2-deficient mice. This exacerbation of the pathology was associated with a reduction in microglial activation indicated by typical morphological features and altered expression of specific markers. Our study confirms that a defect in microglial TREM2 signaling leads to an increase in neuronal tauopathy.

[![](/drf/ifrancoisjacob/PublishingImages/departements-services/MIRCen/tau%20trem2.jpg)](/drf/ifrancoisjacob/PublishingImages/departements-services/MIRCen/tau%20trem2.jpg)

![](/drf/ifrancoisjacob/PublishingImages/departements-services/MIRCen/tau%20trem2.jpg)

## Alternative and Atomic Energies Agency

CEA is a French government-funded technological research organisation in four main areas: low-carbon energies, defense and security, information technologies and health technologies. A prominent player in the European Research Area, it is involved in setting up collaborative projects with many partners around the world.

enriched_points.expanded_content: In vitro models reveal critical neuron-glia interactions, essential for understanding regional vulnerabilities in neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
These models facilitate drug testing, allowing researchers to assess neuroprotective effects and therapeutic efficacy on neuronal survival against neurodegeneration.
Despite their utility, in vitro models often lack the full complexity of brain architecture, limiting their ability to mimic true neurodegenerative conditions.
